sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: The management of Kaposi sarcoma (KS) is guided by principles centered on immune optimization, avoidance of additional immunosuppression, and a multimodal, patient-centered approach. Treatment goals are primarily palliative and focused on disease control, as complete remissions are rare, especially in advanced disease. Individual KS lesions are often distinct clones arising from common risk factors like immunosuppression and persistent KSHV infection, rather than metastases, so treating existing lesions may not prevent new ones [KS-B]. The approach varies by KS type (HIV-associated, classic, iatrogenic, endemic), disease extent, and patient performance status. For HIV-related KS, co-management with an HIV specialist is critical to optimize antiretroviral therapy (ART) and immune reconstitution [KS-B]. Glucocorticoids in any formulation are generally contraindicated due to association with KS progression, except in life-threatening conditions [KS-B, 1-3].

nodes.label: Co-manage with HIV specialist (if HIV-related) and review DDIs
Assess disease extent (limited cutaneous vs advanced)

cards.title: Intent
cards.detail: Intent: Disease control and palliation. Patient group: All patients with KS, particularly those with symptomatic or advanced disease. Approach: For limited cutaneous disease, minimally invasive local therapies are preferred. For advanced disease, systemic therapy is used with the goal of reducing symptoms, lymphedema, or organ threat. Treatment is continued until toxicity or response plateau; maintenance beyond plateau is not recommended [KS-B].
